content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


April 10, 1990


Fred Arjani
Republican Party of Los Angeles County
511 Mission Street
South Pasadena, CA  91030

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-90-195

Dear Mr. Arjani:

     You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

     1.     Does the Act impose contribution limits upon the
Republican Central Committee of Los Angeles County (the
"Committee") for the following expenditures which are not made at the candidate's
particular candidate but which identify a
particular candidate but which are not made at the candidate's
behest:

     a.     Voter registration and an absentee voter program?

     b.     Campaign materials (buttons, bumper stickers, etc.)
provided free to voters?

     2.     Does the Act impose contribution limits upon the
Committee for direct mailings which urge recipients to vote for
the "Republican candidate?" The mailings would not identify a
candidate by name or be made at the candidate's behest.

     3.     May the Committee pay expenditures referred to in
Questions 1 and 2 above from its bank account established for non-
candidate contribution purposes?




___________________________
¹    Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Fred Arjani
Our File No. A-90-195
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

1. and 2. Based upon the facts presented, the expenditures
referred to in Question 1 and 2 are "independent expenditures" and
are not subject to the Act's contribution limits. However, please
review the following analysis carefully to ensure that such
expenditures do not meet the Act's definition of a contribution
and thus become subject to the Act's contribution limitations.

3. Independent expenditures may be paid from the
Committee's bank account established for non-candidate
contribution purposes as long as no other candidate has made
contributions to that account.

FACTS

The Republican Central Committee of Los Angeles County
intends to make expenditures for several types of communications
to voters. None of the communications will be made at a
candidate's behest. Certain communications will identify a
candidate by name. Others will only contain a reference to the
"Republican candidate." The Committee has established separate
bank accounts designated for candidate contributions and non-
candidate contribution purposes.

ANALYSIS

Section 82025 defines an "expenditure" as any payment made
for political purposes. Regulation 18225(a)(2)(C) states that a
payment is "made for political purposes" when it is made by an
official committee of a political party, "including a state
central committee, county central committee, assembly district
committee or any subcommittee of such committee."

An expenditure made at the behest of a candidate is a
contribution under the Act. (Section 82015.) Regulation 18215(b)
states that a payment is "made at the behest" of a candidate when
it is "made under the control or at the direction of, in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with, or at
the request or suggestion of a candidate, controlled committee,

Fred Arjani
Our File No. A-90-195
Page 3


official committee of a political party, or organization formed or
existing primarily for political purposes."2/

An expenditure becomes an "independent expenditure" under the
Act if it is made:

     ...in connection with a communication which expressly
     advocates the election or defeat of a clearly identified
     candidate ... but which is not made to or at the behest of
     the affected candidate...

                                                    Section 82031.

     Based on the facts presented in your letter, the Committee
will not be making expenditures at the behest of the candidate
identified in the voter registration program and campaign
materials. Therefore, such expenditures meet the Act's definition
of "independent expenditure." Likewise, a mailing urging voters
to vote for the "Republican candidate" meets the Act's definition
of an independent expenditure. (Craigie Advice Letter, No. A-89-
236; copy enclosed.).3/

     Sections 85301 and 85303 limit the amount that persons and
committees may contribute to candidates for elective office.
These limitations apply only to contributions to candidates. They
do not limit independent expenditures made by any person or
committee. The United States Supreme Court has ruled that it is
unconstitutional to impose limitations on the amount of
independent expenditures any individual or committee may make.
(Federal Election Com'n v. Nat. Conservative Pol. Action Committee
(1978) 435 U.S. 765; Buckley v. Valeo (1976) 424 U.S. 1.)




_________________________
2/    At its December 1989 meeting the Commission amended
Regulation 18215 to exclude certain expenditures made at the
behest of a candidate by a political party from the definition of
"contribution." The expenditures must be in connection with voter
registration or get-out-the-vote activities which do not clearly
identify the candidate or expressly advocate the election or
defeat of the candidate or a rival candidate. The amended
regulation will go into effect on May 1, 1990. A copy of the
regulation, with the amended language, is enclosed.

3/    Federal Election Commission v. Furgatch (9th Cir. 1987) 807
F. 2d 857, 864, states that a communication expressly advocates
the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate if, "when
read as a whole and with limited reference to external events," is
"susceptible of no other reasonable interpretation but as an
exhortation to vote for or against a specific candidate."

Fred Arjani
Our File No. A-90-195
Page 4


     Please note the Committee may be subject to the notice
requirements of Sections 85600 and 85604.  Section 85600 requires
the printing of a specific disclaimer on independent expenditures
for a mass mailing4/ which supports or opposes any legislative
candidate.  Section 85604 requires notification of the Commission
and certain candidates when an independent expenditure of more
than $10,000 is made that supports or opposes any legislative
candidate.

     You asked in Question 3 whether these independent
expenditures may be paid from a bank account established for non-
candidate contribution purposes.

     Section 85302 limits the amount of contributions that
committees may receive to make contributions to candidates.
Regulation 18535 (copy enclosed) permits political parties to
receive contributions in excess of the $2,500 contribution limit
of Section 85302 as long as the contributions are spent for
purposes other than to make contributions to candidates and the
bank account is separate from accounts used to make contributions
to candidates.  Thus, the committee generally may use funds in
this separate bank account to make independent expenditures.

     However, please note that Section 85202 restricts a
candidate's use of campaign funds.

     ...(b)  All contributions deposited into the campaign account
shall be deemed to be held in trust for expenses associated
with the election of the candidate to the specific office for
which the candidate has stated, pursuant to Section 85200,
that he or she intends to seek or expenses associated with
holding that office...

     The trust provision in Section 85202 restricts a candidate
from using his or her campaign funds for a purpose not
associated with the election of the candidate.  Therefore, the
Committee can make independent expenditures on behalf of a
candidate from a bank account designated for non-candidate
contribution purposes as long as no other candidate has made
contributions to that bank account.


4/  "Mass mailing" means over 200 substantially similar pieces of
mail.  (Section 82041.5.)

Fred Arjani
Our File No. A-90-195
Page 5


Please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5662 if you
have additional questions.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: Lynda Cassady
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures

3. May the Committee pay expenditures referred to in Questions 1 and 2 above from its bank account established for non-candidate contribution purposes?

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

sections.analysis:
Section 82025 defines an "expenditure" as any payment made for political purposes. Regulation 18225(a)(2)(C) states that a payment is "made for political purposes" when it is made by an official committee of a political party, "including a state central committee, county central committee, assembly district committee or any subcommittee of such committee."

An expenditure made at the behest of a candidate is a contribution under the Act. (Section 82015.) Regulation 18215(b) states that a payment is "made at the behest" of a candidate when it is "made under the control or at the direction of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with, or at the request or suggestion of a candidate, controlled committee,

official committee of a political party, or organization formed or existing primarily for political purposes."2/

An expenditure becomes an "independent expenditure" under the Act if it is made:

...in connection with a communication which expressly advocates the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate ... but which is not made to or at the behest of the affected candidate...

Section 82031.

Based on the facts presented in your letter, the Committee will not be making expenditures at the behest of the candidate identified in the voter registration program and campaign materials. Therefore, such expenditures meet the Act's definition of "independent expenditure." Likewise, a mailing urging voters to vote for the "Republican candidate" meets the Act's definition of an independent expenditure. (Craigie Advice Letter, No. A-89-236; copy enclosed.).3/

Sections 85301 and 85303 limit the amount that persons and committees may contribute to candidates for elective office. These limitations apply only to contributions to candidates. They do not limit independent expenditures made by any person or committee. The United States Supreme Court has ruled that it is unconstitutional to impose limitations on the amount of independent expenditures any individual or committee may make. (Federal Election Com'n v. Nat. Conserv. Pol. Action Committee (1978) 435 U.S. 765; Buckley v. Valeo (1976) 424 U.S. 1.)

2/ At its December 1989 meeting the Commission amended Regulation 18215 to exclude certain expenditures made at the behest of a candidate by a political party from the definition of "contribution." The expenditures must be in connection with voter registration or get-out-the-vote activities which do not clearly identify the candidate or expressly advocate the election or defeat of the candidate or a rival candidate. The amended regulation will go into effect on May 1, 1990. A copy of the regulation, with the amended language, is enclosed.

3/ Federal Election Commission v. Furgatch (9th Cir. 1987) 807 F. 2d 857, 864, states that a communication expressly advocates the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate if, "when read as a whole and with limited reference to external events," is "susceptible of no other reasonable interpretation but as an exhortation to vote for or against a specific candidate."

Please note the Committee may be subject to the notice requirements of Sections 85600 and 85604. Section 85600 requires the printing of a specific disclaimer on independent expenditures for a mass mailing4/ which supports or opposes any legislative candidate. Section 85604 requires notification of the Commission and certain candidates when an independent expenditure of more than $10,000 is made that supports or opposes any legislative candidate.

You asked in Question 3 whether these independent expenditures may be paid from a bank account established for non-candidate contribution purposes.

Section 85302 limits the amount of contributions that committees may receive to make contributions to candidates. Regulation 18535 (copy enclosed) permits political parties to receive contributions in excess of the $2,500 contribution limit of Section 85302 as long as the contributions are spent for purposes other than to make contributions to candidates and the bank account is separate from accounts used to make contributions to candidates. Thus, the committee generally may use funds in this separate bank account to make independent expenditures.

However, please note that Section 85202 restricts a candidate's use of campaign funds.

...(b) All contributions deposited into the campaign account shall be deemed to be held in trust for expenses associated with the election of the candidate to the specific office for which the candidate has stated, pursuant to Section 85200, that he or she intends to seek or expenses associated with holding that office...

The trust provision in Section 85202 restricts a candidate from using his or her campaign funds for a purpose not associated with the election of the candidate. Therefore, the Committee can make independent expenditures on behalf of a candidate from a bank account designated for non-candidate contribution purposes as long as no other candidate has made contributions to that bank account.

4/ "Mass mailing" means over 200 substantially similar pieces of mail. (Section 82041.5.)

Fred Arjani  
Our Page 5

a. Voter registration and an absentee voter program?

b. Campaign materials (buttons, bumper stickers, etc.) provided free to voters?

2. Does the Act impose contribution limits upon the Committee for direct mailings which urge recipients to vote for the "Republican candidate?" The mailings would not identify a candidate by name or be made at the candidate's behest.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

CONCLUSION: 1. and 2. Based upon the facts presented, the expenditures referred to in Question 1 and 2 are "independent expenditures" and are not subject to the Act's contribution limits. However, please review the following analysis carefully to ensure that such expenditures do not meet the Act's definition of a contribution and thus become subject to the Act's contribution limitations.

3. Independent expenditures may be paid from the Committee's bank account established for non-candidate contribution purposes as long as no other candidate has made contributions to that account.
